elements.sent-2411-001: Objective: Enhancing the reliability of myoelectric controllers that decode motor intent is a pressing challenge in the field of bionic prosthetics.
elements.sent-2411-002: State-of-the-art research has mostly focused on Supervised Learning (SL) techniques to tackle this problem.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, obtaining high-quality labeled data that accurately represents muscle activity during daily usage remains difficult.
elements.sent-2411-004: We investigate the potential of Reinforcement Learning (RL) to further improve the decoding of human motion intent by incorporating usage-based data.
elements.sent-2411-005: Methods: The starting point of our method is a SL control policy, pretrained on a static recording of electromyographic (EMG) ground truth data.
elements.sent-2411-006: We then apply RL to fine-tune the pretrained classifier with dynamic EMG data obtained during interaction with a game environment developed for this work.
elements.sent-2411-007: We conducted real-time experiments to evaluate our approach and achieved significant improvements in human-in-the-loop performance.
elements.sent-2411-008: Results: The method effectively predicts simultaneous finger movements, leading to a two-fold increase in decoding accuracy during gameplay and a 39\% improvement in a separate motion test.
elements.sent-2411-009: Conclusion: By employing RL and incorporating usage-based EMG data during fine-tuning, our method achieves significant improvements in accuracy and robustness.
elements.sent-2411-010: Significance: These results showcase the potential of RL for enhancing the reliability of myoelectric controllers, of particular importance for advanced bionic limbs.
elements.sent-2411-011: See our project page for visual demonstrations: \href{https://sites.google.com/view/bionic-limb-rl {sites.google.com/view/bionic-limb-rl .}}
elements.sent-2411-012: Roughly 58 million people were living with limb amputation worldwide, as of 2017 \cite{mcdonald2021global .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Even though extensive research efforts have gone into developing better prosthetic devices, these are still far from human-level performance.
elements.sent-2411-014: As a consequence, the adoption of prostheses is effortful, and abandonment rates are high, with one of the main factors being a lack of reliable functionality \cite{cordella2016literature, smail2021comfort .}
elements.sent-2411-015: The performance of a prosthesis greatly depends on the accuracy with which the user's motion intent can be decoded to determine the appropriate bionic joint actuation.
elements.sent-2411-016: There are several biological signals that can be used to determine human motion intent, such as electroencephalography (EEG) or electromyography (EMG) measurements.
elements.sent-2411-017: The latter has been shown to be more practical, is more widely adopted \cite{jiang2010myoelectric, li2021gesture , and is therefore used in this work.}
elements.sent-2411-018: In myoelectric control, there are two main approaches used to decode motor intent for actuating bionic limbs: (i) direct control and (ii) pattern recognition control.
elements.sent-2411-019: Direct control, involves mapping individual muscle signals — often on a one-to-one basis — to a specific bionic joint.
elements.sent-2411-020: When a mapped muscle contracts and its signal value surpasses a predetermined threshold, the corresponding bionic joint is actuated.
elements.sent-2411-021: This approach is appealing due to its simplicity however, the few signals available from people with amputation are often not easily separable and thus cannot always be mapped in a one-to-one fashion.
elements.sent-2411-022: In practice, direct control frequently involves mapping only two antagonistic muscle groups, with mode switching employed to control more than one Degree of Freedom (DOF), making it impractical to use as the number of DOFs grow.
elements.sent-2411-023: Pattern recognition control, leverages Machine Learning (ML) to train a function approximator which automatically maps biological signals to an intended motion \cite{kuiken2016comparison .}
elements.sent-2411-024: This control method thus bypasses the need to solve the signal separability problem through hand engineering efforts.
elements.sent-2411-025: According to Mereu et al. \cite{mereu2021control , this approach is preferred over direct control by people with amputation due to its more intuitive usability.}

Each movement is labeled as $m_i$ with $i=0,\ldots,12$ and with $m_0$ referring to `Rest'.} \label{fig:movements} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-026: Though pattern recognition control has shown promising results, online usability of ML-based controllers remains limited, especially for hand gestures \cite{li2021gesture .}
elements.sent-2411-027: Many ML approaches have been tested to decode motor intent, from classical algorithms such as support vector machines \cite{oskoei2008support , to deep learning algorithms using Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs).}
elements.sent-2411-028: Most work on pattern recognition control has focused on Supervised Learning (SL) with different ANN architectures, such as feed-forward \cite{hudgins1993new , recurrent \cite{williams2022recurrent, luu2022artificial , convolutional \cite{cha2022study and transformers \cite{godoy2022electromyography .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-029: However, much of this research is confined to offline evaluations, which does not guarantee the same online performance, with a human in the loop \cite{ortiz2015offline .}
elements.sent-2411-030: We, on the other hand, investigate the potential of Reinforcement Learning (RL) methods to increase the online performance of a bionic limb controller, by fine-tuning an initial ANN policy trained via SL.
elements.sent-2411-031: Other works have attempted to improve ML-based motor intent decoding by combining multiple signals through sensor fusion.
elements.sent-2411-032: Mouchoux et al. \cite{mouchoux2021artificial , propose integrating inertial units and a camera to obtain context information to improve the performance of a myoelectric controller, in an augmented reality setup.}
elements.sent-2411-033: In contrast, our work aims to maximize the utilization of EMG signals, without additional sensors or hardware, making it a more affordable strategy.
elements.sent-2411-034: Moreover, recent research has explored procedures to improve the EMG signal quality, by implanting electrodes directly in the muscles \cite{vaskov2022surgically instead of placing them on the surface of the skin, which further encourages pursuing EMG-based strategies.}
elements.sent-2411-035: A key limitation of ML-based control, is the need to collect labeled EMG data.
elements.sent-2411-036: This typically involves instructing the user to repeatedly contract their muscles to generate activation patterns for each movement that is to be learned during a lengthy recording session.
elements.sent-2411-037: Collecting such ground-truth data becomes increasingly cumbersome as the number of DOFs grows.
elements.sent-2411-038: The problem is exacerbated when simultaneous movements are also considered (e.g. open hand while rotating wrist) since each movement combination needs to be present in the data.
elements.sent-2411-039: Tommasi et al. \cite{tommasi2012improving proposed using transfer learning, with data from several able-bodied subjects, to reduce the amount of data needed when training a control policy for a new subject.}
elements.sent-2411-040: This approach presents challenges when applied to people with amputations due to substantial variations in residual muscle profiles.
elements.sent-2411-041: Furthermore, data from such recording sessions can differ from natural muscle activity during daily usage.
elements.sent-2411-042: This discrepancy between training data and real-life usage can further hinder robust control and thereby impact the functionality of ML-based prostheses.
elements.sent-2411-043: To address these problems, we propose training a control policy within an interactive game setting, which can be more representative of daily-life scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-044: This also helps reduce the length of the initial recording session, since additional data can be collected during gameplay.
elements.sent-2411-045: In this work, we present an RL-based approach which leverages data collected in an interactive game environment, to close the gap between offline evaluations and online performance.
elements.sent-2411-046: By having users interact with a game through a myoelectric policy, we can get valuable human feedback about its performance.
elements.sent-2411-047: It then becomes possible to iteratively improve the policy towards better online performance directly, rather than only trying to copy recorded behaviors by minimizing the offline error, as in SL approaches.
elements.sent-2411-048: We refer to this iterative improvement of a pretrained policy as fine-tuning, where the purpose is to align the learning objective towards better performance on real usage data, with the human in the loop.
elements.sent-2411-049: While we present a specific case study, our approach can be generalized to any myoelectric control task from which a reward signal can be derived.
elements.sent-2411-050: To validate our methods, we developed a simplified game environment which allows for a quantifiable measure of improvement.
elements.sent-2411-051: The environment is inspired by Guitar Hero which requires precise timing, duration, and motion control, much like everyday movements.
elements.sent-2411-052: The task was developed especially with a simultaneous finger control setting in mind, representative of common movement and grasp patterns encountered in daily life.
elements.sent-2411-053: Our online experimental results demonstrate the efficacy of RL in improving the decoding of motor intent across 15 subjects, with a more than two-fold increase in normalized cumulative reward.
elements.sent-2411-054: The results are further validated through testing on a separate task, also revealing a significant increase~in performance.
elements.sent-2411-055: We contextualize our motor intent decoding approach within the broader landscape of advancements in this field, categorizing recent developments into three key areas: a) network architectures, b) data quality and quantity, and c) learning methodologies.
elements.sent-2411-056: We review relevant work in each category, highlighting connections to our proposed method.
elements.sent-2411-057: For instance, Bakircioglu and Özkurt \cite{bakirciouglu2020classification performed offline tests with a CNN and compared it with a feed-forward fully-connected neural network (FFNN) on 6 different hand movements.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Chen et al. \cite{cha2022study showed that using a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) classification head after a Convolutional neural network (CNN) encoder slightly surpassed a fully-connected classification head.}
elements.sent-2411-059: Zbinden et al. \cite{zbinden2024deep , utilizing the same setup as our study, compared fully-connected neural networks with a CNN and Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN) in online experiments.}
elements.sent-2411-060: While it is conceivable that more sophisticated network architectures could further enhance motor intent decoding, they also introduce increased complexity.
elements.sent-2411-061: Moreover, we posit that data collection and training methods are at least as crucial as network architecture, yet these areas remain underexplored.

elements.sent-2411-062: Nevertheless, work by CTRL-labs at Meta Reality Labs \cite{ctrl2024generic also showed that additional personalized fine-tuning can improve performance.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Moreover, iterative online data collection with fine-tuning has been shown to facilitate learning control tasks in sequence labeling \cite{ross2011reduction and myoelectric control \cite{gijsberts2014stable .}}
elements.sent-2411-064: Building upon the latter findings, we examine how iterative fine-tuning using a gameplay-based approach can improve a policy in an engaging way.
elements.sent-2411-065: In other words, we investigate how data quality impacts learning.
elements.sent-2411-066: Transfer learning for EMG control has shown promising results in recent work to enable effective usage of additional data.
elements.sent-2411-067: For example, Ketykó et al. \cite{ketyko2019domain and Tommasi et al. \cite{tommasi2012improving demonstrated improved generalization across sessions and subjects through fine-tuning on target data.}}
elements.sent-2411-068: Chen et al. \cite{chen2020hand demonstrated that transfer learning is also beneficial when the target set contains movements that where not present in the source set.}
elements.sent-2411-069: Other work has eliminated the need for labeled source data in domain adaptation \cite{du2017surface, cote2021transferable , extending the applicability and amount of potential data for the aforementioned methods.}
elements.sent-2411-070: We diverge from these approaches by exploring the impact of changing the training environment, and thus the training data distribution, towards tasks that resemble daily usage.
elements.sent-2411-071: Furthermore, we propose using reinforcement learning to facilitate training without requiring labeled data.
elements.sent-2411-072: While we solely investigate the benefit of iterative online fine-tuning with RL, it is possible to integrate transfer learning to leverage usage of additional offline data.

elements.sent-2411-074: Even though EMG classification via SL has been extensively studied, RL methods have received limited exploration.
elements.sent-2411-075: Nevertheless, some promising approaches have emerged in the literature that we want to highlight.
elements.sent-2411-076: Pilarski et al. \cite{pilarski2011online proposed an RL method that predicts arm movements using EMG and robot state information.}
elements.sent-2411-077: The agent learned to match the human arm angle using velocity-based control.
elements.sent-2411-078: They initially guided the learning through a reward based on proximity to the desired angle and, subsequently through sparse human feedback.
elements.sent-2411-079: Similarly, Vasan et al. \cite{vasan2017learning presented a system that allows for the control of 3 simultaneous DOFs, of a prosthetic limb.}
elements.sent-2411-080: This was achieved by employing RL, to train an ANN while recording EMG data of hand movements.
elements.sent-2411-081: Their method was able to predict proportionality for each DOF and was successfully tested on three able-bodied subjects.
elements.sent-2411-082: While these studies demonstrate the feasibility of directly predicting proportional values, our approach focuses on predicting movement intention, which we believe to be more stable and easier to tune for home use.
elements.sent-2411-083: To the best of our knowledge, employing RL for motion intent decoding in this context has not been done before.
elements.sent-2411-084: When designing a myoelectric controller, a key choice is to decide on the specific movements to control.
elements.sent-2411-085: This decision hinges on the participant's amputation level; more proximal limb loss precludes control of movements tied to now-absent muscles.
elements.sent-2411-086: Although our method is broadly applicable, we focus on finger movements corresponding to common grasp patterns, shown in Fig. \ref{fig:movements .}
elements.sent-2411-087: Incongruent articulations (flex + extend combinations) are not selected as there is evidence that natural control of such motions is not simultaneous \cite{rosenbaum2009human .}
elements.sent-2411-088: This selection of finger movements would be feasible for people with trans-radial amputation.
elements.sent-2411-089: Alternatively, they would be reasonable for patients who have lost the limb more proximally but underwent nerve transfer surgery to create additional myoelectric sites \cite{osborn2021extended, zbinden2023a .}
elements.sent-2411-090: To acquire EMG signals, eight surface electrode pairs are placed along two rings around the forearm of the participant, with an additional electrode for ground, as illustrated in Fig. \ref{fig:setup .}
elements.sent-2411-091: The electrodes are placed in a bipolar configuration, i.e. for each channel, two adjacent signals are subtracted from one another to reduce noise.
elements.sent-2411-092: The upper ring consists of one electrode pair targeting the extensor carpi ulnaris and three additional equally spaced pairs around the proximal side of the forearm.
elements.sent-2411-093: The lower ring includes two electrode pairs targeting the flexor pollicis longus and extensor indicis, and two equally spaced pairs around the distal side of the forearm.
elements.sent-2411-094: Finally, a single electrode placed on the ulnar styloid serves as ground reference.
elements.sent-2411-095: Surface EMG signals are captured at a sampling rate of 1000 Hz and filtered using analog and digital filters.
elements.sent-2411-096: These consist of an analog low-pass filter at 500 Hz, a digital butterworth high-pass filter at 20 Hz, and a second-order notch filter at 50 Hz.
elements.sent-2411-097: The EMG data stream of the eight channels is split with a sliding window approach, into 200 ms windows with 150 ms overlap, equating to an update frequency of 20 Hz.
elements.sent-2411-098: While there have been works which use raw EMG data to directly train an ANN \cite{zia2018multiday , such high-dimensional data can make learning more challenging when limited data is available and it increases computational load.}
elements.sent-2411-099: A common approach to overcome the problem is to extract a set of four features proposed by Hudgins et al. \cite{hudgins1993new from the windowed EMG data.}
elements.sent-2411-100: The features in question are: mean absolute value (MAV), waveform length in time-domain (TWL), number of zero crossings (ZC) and slope changes (SLPCH).
elements.sent-2411-101: In our myoelectric control scheme, the input to the policy is the stacked vector of these features for each channel, as shown in Fig. \ref{fig:setup .}
elements.sent-2411-102: Note how for each DOF (Thumb, Index and Middle, see Fig. \ref{fig:movements ), there are two movements (Flex and Extend).}
elements.sent-2411-103: Clinically, sequential myoelectric control is prevalent.
elements.sent-2411-104: This means that for each possible DOF that a prosthesis can actuate, only one can be active at any given time ($m_1$ to $m_6$).
elements.sent-2411-105: In such cases, the ML problem is formulated as a simple classification task.
elements.sent-2411-106: However, since dexterous manipulation requires simultaneous actions, there have been attempts to control multiple DOFs at the same time \cite{jiang2008extracting, zbinden2024deep, ameri2018real .}
elements.sent-2411-107: The most straightforward approach is to treat each movement combination as a new class.
elements.sent-2411-108: Alternatively, we formulate the ML problem as a multi-label classification task.
elements.sent-2411-109: To that end, each movement is encoded into a binary vector $m_i\in \{0, 1\}^{2\cdot{\text{DOF \raisebox{0.05ex { + 1}$, with ${\text{DOF =3$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-110: For example, simultaneous thumb and index flexion is encoded as: \begin{equation}m_{8} = \left[ \begin{array}{cccccccccc} \undermat{\text{Thumb}}{0 1} \undermat{\text{Index}}{0 1} \undermat{\text{Middle}}{0 0} \undermat{\text{Rest}}{\! 0 \!} \end{array} \right] \label{eq:binary_vector}\end{equation} The control policy is chosen to be a relatively small feed-forward, fully-connected (6 hidden layers with ReLU activation, each with 128 neurons) ANN architecture (see \cite{zbinden2024deep for more details).}
elements.sent-2411-111: Note that our methodology is versatile and can be employed on any network architecture.
elements.sent-2411-112: Considering our primary objective of testing with individuals who have undergone amputation, we strategically selected a network architecture that can be seamlessly integrated into a take-home embedded device, facilitating a smooth transition from research to practical implementation.
elements.sent-2411-113: The output layer has a sigmoid activation function, outputting values between $[0,1]$.
elements.sent-2411-114: At test time, outputs are rounded to be exactly $\{0, 1\}$.
elements.figure-2411-003: \begin{center} \centerline{\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/block_diagram.pdf}} \caption{The proposed RL framework consists of obtaining EMG signals from users, that are given to the policy to perform actions in an environment. Further note that the reward signal is only used during RL training and not once the policy is deployed.} \label{fig:rl-framework} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-115: The idea behind RL is to learn a control policy from trial-and-error while interacting with the environment which provides a reward signal (see Fig. \ref{fig:rl-framework ).}
elements.sent-2411-116: More formally, RL problems are formulated as Markov Decision Processes (MDPs).
elements.sent-2411-117: In this work, we consider an episodic MDP setting, defined as a tuple $(\mathcal{S ,\mathcal{A ,p,r, \gamma)$, where $r: \mathcal{S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is a reward function and $\gamma\in (0,1]$ is the discount factor.}}}
elements.sent-2411-118: The probability density function $p(s_{t+1 | s_t, a_t)$ represents the probability of transitioning to state $s_{t+1}$, given the current state $s_t$ and action $a_t$, with $s_t, s_{t+1 \in \mathcal{S}$ and $a_t \in \mathcal{A}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-119: We aim to learn an ANN policy $\pi_\theta(s_t)=a_t$, i.e. the actor, parameterized by $\theta$.
elements.sent-2411-120: The long-term objective function is defined by the return, which is the sum of discounted future rewards: $G_t = \sum_{k=t ^{T \gamma^{k-t r (s_k, a_k)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-121: RL algorithms aim to maximize the expected return conditioned on states, i.e. the state-value $V(s),$ or state-action pairs, i.e. the action-value $Q(s,a)$.
elements.sent-2411-122: In deep RL $Q_\phi(s,a)$, also referred to as the critic, is modelled by an ANN parameterized by $\phi$.
elements.sent-2411-123: Typically in actor-critic methods, the actor is updated such that $Q(s,a)$ is maximized: \begin{equation}\theta_{k+1} = \text{arg}\max_{\theta} \mathbb{E} \left[ Q_\phi (s, \pi_{\theta_k} (s) ) \right]\end{equation}.
elements.subsection-2411-001: RL with Human in the Loop
elements.sent-2411-124: For our application, the RL agent is a combination of the human and the ANN policy.
elements.sent-2411-125: The former contracts their muscles to execute an intended movement, and the latter maps the recorded EMG signals to the correct movement.
elements.sent-2411-126: Collaboration becomes essential as no single entity can solve tasks independently.
elements.sent-2411-127: Online RL presents unique challenges when humans are involved.
elements.sent-2411-128: Firstly, participants having to adapt to policy changes can lead to a less satisfactory experience due to inconsistency between policies.
elements.sent-2411-129: Secondly, the interaction between the human and the environment needs to be real-time, since significant lags between the commands of the human and the game would lead to actions $a_t$ in response to earlier states $s_{t-l}$, where $l$ is the lag.
elements.sent-2411-130: Applying online RL could slow down the game interface, creating such lags.
elements.sent-2411-131: An alternative that has been gaining interest in the literature is offline RL \cite{levine2020offline , which aims to learn from a static dataset, $\mathcal{D}$.}
elements.sent-2411-132: This solves the aforementioned problems but brings its own challenges.
elements.sent-2411-133: Because pure offline RL assumes no additional online data collection, it usually cannot reach acceptable online results without further fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-134: The recent Advantage Weighted Actor-Critic (AWAC) algorithm proposed by Nair et al. \cite{nair2020awac aims to accelerate such online fine-tuning with offline datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-135: AWAC trains an off-policy critic and an actor with an implicit policy constraint.
elements.sent-2411-136: This leads to the modified policy update, described as: \begin{equation}\theta_{k+1} = \text{arg}\max_\theta \mathbb{E} \left[ \log \pi_\theta \, \text{exp} \left( \frac{1}{\lambda} A \left(s, \pi_{\theta_k}(s)\right)\right) \right]\end{equation} where $ A \left(s, a \right)=Q_\phi(s,a)-V(s)$, is the advantage function and $\lambda$ is the Lagrangian multiplier for the constraint.
elements.sent-2411-137: By implicitly constraining the actor to stay close to the actions observed in the data, this algorithm was shown to be able to both effectively train offline and continue improving with experience, on real-world robotic problems.
elements.sent-2411-138: Given these particular characteristics, we opted to use AWAC to train the myoelectric control policy, but our method can be combined with other offline RL algorithms.

elements.sent-2411-140: The action space $\mathcal{A}\in \{0, 1\}^{7}$ is the binary vector described in equation \eqref{eq:binary_vector .}
elements.sent-2411-141: We select the discount factor to be $\gamma = 0.89$, through a hyperparameter search (see Appendix \ref{sec:hyperparameter for more details).}

elements.sent-2411-217: The most straightforward way to measure gameplay performance differences between policies, is to record the return of each episode/repetition.
elements.sent-2411-218: The undiscounted return (cumulative reward) is normalized based on the range of the game score, presented in Section \ref{subsec:rl_formulation : \begin{equation}\bar{G}_0 = \frac{\sum_{t=0}^{T} r (s_t, a_t) + 2740}{3940}\end{equation} where $t$ and $T$ are the current and terminal state's indices, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-219: Note that the normalized return is only used during gameplay and not considered during a Motion Test.
elements.sent-2411-220: To further evaluate a policy's capability of decoding motor intent, we also calculate the EMR and the F1 macro scores.
elements.sent-2411-221: In the context of ML classification tasks, the EMR corresponds to the classification accuracy, however, in a multi-label setting, this terminology can be ambiguous since there can be partially correct classifications.
elements.sent-2411-222: For this reason, we refer to the EMR instead, which measures the proportion of correct predictions out of all predictions made by the classifier.
elements.sent-2411-223: When computing the EMR, even partially correct classifications are considered completely incorrect, emphasizing the requirement for precise and accurate predictions.
elements.sent-2411-224: The EMR is defined as: \begin{equation}\text{EMR} = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathbbm{1} ( a_t = a_t^*)\end{equation} where $\mathbbm{1}$ is the indicator function, $a_t$ the movement predicted and $a_t^*$ the correct movement at time $t$, which is determined by the song being played.
elements.sent-2411-225: Nevertheless, if some but not all of the target labels are predicted, one could argue that this is more accurate than a case in which none of the target labels are predicted.
elements.sent-2411-226: Therefore we also consider the F1 score for evaluation, which offers a class-wise assessment of performance and is a commonly used indicator for multi-label classification tasks \cite{yang1999evaluation .}
elements.sent-2411-227: The F1 score for each class, $i=1,\ldots,N$, is computed as: \begin{equation}\text{F1}_i= \frac{\text{TP}_i}{\text{TP}_i + 0.5 (\text{FP}_i+\text{FN}_i)}\end{equation} where TP denotes True Positives, FP represents False Positives, and FN corresponds to False Negatives.
elements.sent-2411-228: The F1 macro score is obtained by taking the macro average: \begin{equation}\text{F1}_{\text{macro}}= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{F1}_i\end{equation} Together, these evaluation metrics provide comprehensive insights into the effectiveness of the policy for multi-label classification tasks.

elements.subsection-2411-009: How do our findings relate to the broader landscape?
elements.sent-2411-261: Here, we aim to contextualize our results in relation to recent advances in EMG signal classification.
elements.sent-2411-262: When focusing on the network architecture, Bakircioglu and Özkurt \cite{bakirciouglu2020classification outperformed a FFNN with a CNN by $4\%$ for 6 different hand movements in offline tests.}
elements.sent-2411-263: Chen et al. \cite{cha2022study showed that an LSTM classification head after a CNN encoder slightly surpassed a fully connected classification head (97.34 \% vs 93.32 \% accuracy).}
elements.sent-2411-264: Similarly, Zbinden et al. \cite{zbinden2024deep showed that replacing a six-layer FFNN with a CNN (which surpassed a TCN) resulted in an F1 macro score enhancement of 5.7\% in an online Motion Test.}
elements.sent-2411-265: Our findings, which demonstrate improvements of up to 250\% in gameplay and 35\% of F1 macro score in a Motion Test using the same six-layer fully-connected network as in \cite{zbinden2024deep underscore the significance of data collection and learning method instead of exclusively focusing on the network architecture.}
elements.sent-2411-266: Chen et al. \cite{chen2020hand demonstrated substantial gains in accuracy using their proposed transfer learning approach.}
elements.sent-2411-267: Specifically, they achieved an enhancement of 67.8\% when employing a CNN-only architecture and 28.4\% with a CNN+LSTM, compared to plain SL training, in offline evaluations for scenarios where the target labels differ from the source labels.
elements.sent-2411-268: Furthermore, Hannius et al. \cite{hannius2024towards demonstrate a 9 \% improvement in online decoding accuracy by employing Sliced Wasserstein Discrepancy in a limb position domain shift problem.}
elements.sent-2411-269: While transfer learning mainly aims to improve generalization by using additional offline data, our results show that the problem of distributional shift between training and deployment can also be tackled during fine-tuning, as evidenced by our competitive improvements.
elements.sent-2411-270: It is important to note that our method does not replace transfer learning, instead we show that carefully designed fine-tuning can significantly increase decoding performance as well.
elements.sent-2411-271: Further note that our results are not directly comparable to those by Vasan and Pilarski \cite{vasan2017learning as in their RL approach they estimate joint velocities, whereas we focus on motion classification.}
elements.sent-2411-272: A limitation of our study is that we are unable to independently assess the distinct contributions of our game environment and RL training procedure.
elements.sent-2411-273: We leave it as an open issue for future research to explore the independent effects of each component.

elements.sent-2411-317: In this study, we proposed an RL-based procedure to improve the decoding of motion intent with the aim of creating more intuitive and responsive myoelectric controllers.
elements.sent-2411-318: Our approach using an interactive game significantly enhanced the online, human-in-the-loop performance of an ML controller for all evaluation metrics, thus bridging the gap between offline training and real-life usage.
elements.sent-2411-319: Since our results are promising, future investigations will be carried out on individuals with amputation, which will provide valuable insights into the effectiveness of our approach in a clinical setting.
elements.sent-2411-320: Additionally, our method exhibits a versatile nature, allowing for its extension to other serious games that more accurately simulate daily prosthetic use, thereby ensuring a higher level of realism and applicability.
elements.sent-2411-321: Finally, our work opens up avenues for further advancements by incorporating fine-tuning techniques based on human feedback.
elements.sent-2411-322: This has the potential to greatly improve prosthetic functionality as it enables individuals to train on any tasks encountered in their daily lives.

elements.sent-2411-324: We employed a hyperparameter optimization process to find the most suitable parameters for RL training.

elements.sent-2411-326: We simulated playing a song by re-predicting actions of a recorded session with the current policy.
elements.sent-2411-327: This simulation provided the cumulative reward which served as such an offline measure.
elements.sent-2411-328: The policy with the highest simulated return on test data was chosen.
elements.sent-2411-329: We additionally employed the model selection procedure, as described in Section\ref{subsubsec:model_selection , during the search to chose the best model of each individual trial using its training data.}

elements.sent-2411-342: Mutual Information (MI) is a measure of how much information is present in one random variable $X$ about another random variable $Y$.
elements.sent-2411-343: It can be defined as \begin{equation}I(X;Y) = H(X) + H(Y) - H(X,Y)\end{equation} where $\mathbb{H}$ is Shannon's entropy.
elements.sent-2411-344: The MI equates to zero if the two variables are independent and grows in value as the dependence between $X$ and $Y$ increases.
elements.sent-2411-345: To compute the MI, we used a non-parametric method based on entropy estimation from k-nearest neighbors distances, proposed by \cite{kraskov2004estimating, ross2014mutual .}
elements.sent-2411-346: Specifically, we use the scikit-learn implementation \cite{scikitlearn2011 (i.e. mutual\_info\_classif).}
elements.sent-2411-347: For our analysis, we focus on the MI between features and ideal labels, denoted as $\mathbb{I (s;a^*)$.}
elements.sent-2411-348: We first investigate how this value evolves over gameplay repetitions.
elements.sent-2411-349: To this end, we plot the mean MI against the number of repetitions in Fig. \ref{fig:mi_gameplay .}
elements.sent-2411-350: As shown, the MI increases up to repetition 8 and then decreases in repetition 9.
elements.sent-2411-351: Notably, repetition 9 is conducted using policy $\pi_0$, suggesting a potential connection between policy and MI.
elements.sent-2411-352: Given the observed correlation between the MI in repetition 8 and the success of our method (i.e., the MI in repetition 8 appears predictive of the performance of $\pi_8$), we wanted to investigate whether the MI in repetition 9 (using $\pi_0$) is similarly predictive of the performance of $\pi_0$.
elements.sent-2411-353: To address this, we present a comparable plot for repetition 9 in Fig. \ref{fig:mi_sl .}
elements.sent-2411-354: Our results indicate that the gameplay MI using $\pi_0$ is less predictive of its performance compared to $\pi_8$.

elements.sent-2411-355: To investigate if inconsistencies in movements where the cause for unsuccessful training in two cases, we also calculate the Population Stability Index (PSI) \cite{yurdakul2018statistical to further examine how feature distributions drift across each song repetition, for each participant.}
elements.sent-2411-356: We compute the PSI of each feature as: \begin{equation}\text{PSI}(P, Q) = \sum_j^{10} (P_j - Q_j) \cdot \ln\left(\frac{P_j}{Q_j}\right)\end{equation} where $P$ is the base distribution, $Q$ is the target distribution, and $j$ indicates the bin index.
elements.sent-2411-357: To calculate the PSI, we discretize each feature's distribution into 8 bins using a reference distribution $P$.
elements.sent-2411-358: We use median-based binning, starting with an initial split based on the median, followed by further cuts at intervals of ${1 /{3}$ of the standard deviation, resulting in 4 bins on each side.}
elements.sent-2411-359: Additionally, we include a lowest and highest bin with cutoffs at the minimum and maximum of $P$ respectively.
elements.sent-2411-360: Empty edge bins are removed, leaving a final count of 8-10 bins.
elements.sent-2411-361: As the PSI is defined for individual features, we take the mean over the PSI values to quantify the extent of distribution divergence.
